elements.sent-2411-001: The origin of the $A$-type antiferromagnetic ordering—where ferromagnetic layers couple antiferromagnetically-- in the semiconductor altermagnet $\alpha$-MnTe has been a subject of ongoing debate.
elements.sent-2411-002: Experimentally, $\alpha$-MnTe exhibits a nearest-neighbor in-plane ferromagnetic exchange interaction, whereas previous ab initio calculations predicted an antiferromagnetic interaction.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this Letter, we resolve this discrepancy by considering an expanded set of magnetic configurations, which reveals an FM in-plane exchange interaction in agreement with experimental findings.
elements.sent-2411-004: Additionally, we demonstrate that the 10th nearest-neighbor exchange interaction is directionally dependent, inducing a chiral splitting in the magnon bands, as recently observed experimentally.
elements.sent-2411-005: We further show that applying a compressive strain reverses the sign of the in-plane exchange interaction and significantly enhances the spin and chiral splittings of the electronic and magnonic bands, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-006: Our results highlight the critical importance of convergence in the number of magnetic configurations for complex spin interactions in antiferromagnetic materials.
elements.sent-2411-007: Introduction-- Antiferromagnetic (AFM) interactions give rise to a diverse range of AFM classes, ranging from different collinear to various exotic noncollinear and frustrated structures~\cite{RevModPhys.90.015005,Parkin,dal2024antiferromagnetic .}
elements.sent-2411-008: A class of nonrelativistic collinear antiferromagnetic systems with broken combined parity and time-reversal ($\mathcal{PT}$) symmetry, resulting in lifted Kramers’ degeneracy, while preserving the combined crystal rotation and time-reversal symmetry, has recently been identified in various AFM materials both theoretically~\cite{noda2016momentum,naka2019spin,doi:10.7566/JPSJ.88.123702,PhysRevB.99.184432,vsmejkal2020crystal,PhysRevB.102.014422,doi:10.1021/acs.jpcc.1c02653,mazin2021prediction,PhysRevLett.126.127701,PhysRevX.12.040501,PhysRevX.12.011028,PhysRevX.12.031042,PhysRevB.102.014422,PhysRevLett.131.256703,Zunger-review,yuan2023uncovering,2024ApPhL.124c0503Y,PhysRevX.14.011019,S_dequist_2024,Cheong_2024,KIMEL2024172039,https://doi.org/10.1002/adfm.202409327, jungwirth2024,jungwirth2024supefluid3healtermagnets and experimentally~\cite{Krempasky2024,PhysRevLett.132.036702,PhysRevB.109.115102,doi:10.1126/sciadv.adj4883,Reimers_2024, https://doi.org/10.1002/advs.202406529,ding2024largebandsplittinggwavetype .}}
elements.sent-2411-009: This class of spin-split collinear AFM materials has been termed altermagnetism.
elements.sent-2411-010: In altermagnets, despite the absence of net magnetization, the electronic (magnonic) band structures display direction-dependent spin (chiral) splitting within certain regions of the magnetic Brillouin zone.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Figures/crystal_Js-2.pdf} \caption{(a) The crystal structure of $\alpha$-MnTe. We present several important exchange interactions: the 10th n.n. interactions ($J_{10,a}$ and $J_{10,b}$) that lead to altermagnetism, as well as 1st. n.n. $J_1$ and and 2nd n.n. $J_2$. Large purple spheres represent Mn atoms and small cyan spheres represent Te atoms. (b) The 2nd nearest neighbor exchange interaction, $J_2$, plotted against the number of magnetic configurations, starting from 18. Initially, $J_2$ is negative (AFM), but it transitions to positive (FM) and stabilizes as the number of configurations increases. Inset of (b) represents the Heisenberg exchange interactions at ambient pressure $P=0$ and $P=15$ GPa.} \label{fig:MnTe_Js}
elements.sent-2411-011: This material exhibits pronounced magnetostrictive and piezomagnetic properties~\cite{https://doi.org/10.1002/adfm.202305247,PhysRevMaterials.8.L041402 .}
elements.sent-2411-012: The nearest-neighbor (n.n.) Mn-Mn bond is along the interlayer direction while the 2nd n.n.
elements.sent-2411-013: Experimentally, it was shown that the system has an $A$-type AFM structure~\cite{PhysRevB.73.104403,Kriegner_2016,PhysRevB.96.214418,PhysRevLett.133.156702 , with in-plane ferromagnetic (FM) exchange interaction, $J_2 > 0$~\cite{PhysRevB.73.104403,PhysRevLett.133.156702 .}}
elements.sent-2411-014: However, recent density functional theory (DFT) calculations show an AFM exchange interaction $J_2 < 0$~\cite{PhysRevB.107.L100418,PhysRevMaterials.3.025403,https://doi.org/10.1002/apxr.202300050 .}
elements.sent-2411-015: On the other hand, recent inelastic neutron experiment has also found a chiral splitting of magnon bands in this system~\cite{PhysRevLett.133.156702 .}
elements.sent-2411-016: In this Letter, using ab initio calculations to compute the total energy, we resolve the previous discrepancy between DFT and experimental results.
elements.sent-2411-017: Our main fining, summarized in Fig.~\ref{fig:MnTe_Js (b), shows that to find the correct sing for the in-plane exchange coupling $J_2$, one needs to take into account many magnetic configurations.}
elements.sent-2411-018: We also show that the 10th n.n.
elements.sent-2411-019: In addition, we demonstrate that applying compressive strain enhances both spin-split electronic bands and chiral-split magnon bands.
elements.sent-2411-020: Furthermore, the applied pressure changes the sign of \( J_2 \), while still the magnetic ground state remains collinear $A$-type AFM, offering new control over magnetic interactions.
elements.sent-2411-021: Computational methods-- To calculate the electronic band structures, the total energy, and consequently spin interactions, we use the projected augmented wave (PAW) method as implemented in the Vienna Ab initio Simulation Package (VASP)~\cite{PhysRevB.59.1758 .}
elements.sent-2411-022: The plane wave expansion employs a cut-off energy of 550 eV.
elements.sent-2411-023: We apply the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) developed by Perdew, Burke, and Ernzerhof (PBE) to account for electron exchange-correlation energy~\cite{PBE .}
elements.sent-2411-024: Additionally, we include a Hubbard correction of $U = 4$ eV, as estimated in Ref.~\cite{Mosleh2023 , to improve the treatment of electron-electron interactions.}
elements.sent-2411-025: To compute Heisenberg exchange interactions $\Tilde{J _{ij}$, between localized spins $\mathbf{S _{i =S \hat{\mathbf{S _{i}$, where $S=5/2$ is the spin length and $\hat{\mathbf{S _{i}$ denotes the spin direction; we employ an implicit approach by fitting a classical Heisenberg model $\mathcal{H =-\sum_{i<j J_{ij}\hat{\mathbf{S _{i}\cdot\hat{\mathbf{S _{j}$, with ${J _{ij =S^2\Tilde{J _{ij}$; to the total energy derived from calculations of the electronic structure across numerous magnetic configurations~\cite{, Alaei_2023, Mosleh2023, RevModPhys.95.035004 .}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-026: For this, we compute exchange interactions up to the 16th n.n., allowing us to capture the chiral magnon band-splitting, accurately.
elements.sent-2411-027: To optimize computational efficiency, we select the minimal supercell that effectively captures all relevant exchange interactions.
elements.sent-2411-028: For this purpose, we apply the SUPERHEX method~\cite{superhex , recently introduced by some of us.}
elements.sent-2411-029: Using SUPERHEX, we obtain a supercell with just 34 Mn atoms.
elements.sent-2411-030: In contrast, a conventional approach would require a much larger supercell of $5\times5\times4$, which contains 200 Mn atoms.
elements.sent-2411-031: At ambient pressure, we use approximately 60 unique magnetic configurations and apply a least-squares fitting to the Heisenberg Hamiltonian model.
elements.sent-2411-032: Under a compressive pressure of 15 GPa, however, achieving convergence in exchange interactions requires about 120 magnetic configurations.
elements.sent-2411-033: In our DFT calculations, we neglect relativistic spin-orbit coupling effects to concentrate on the primary exchange interactions.
elements.sent-2411-034: Spin-resolved electronic band structure-- In altermagnets, Kramers' degeneracy is lifted, leading to a momentum-dependent splitting of spin subbands in the electronic band structure.
elements.sent-2411-035: The left and right panels in Fig.~\ref{fig:bands_vs_P present the spin-resolved electronic band structure of bulk $\alpha$-MnTe at ambient pressure and $15$~GPa, respectively, along the L-$\Gamma$ path in the Brillouin zone, where the material exhibits the largest spin splitting.}
elements.sent-2411-036: We measure the nonrelativistic spin subband splitting at the $2/3$L k-point to compare its behavior under different pressures.
elements.sent-2411-037: We found a large spin split of $\Delta V_1 = 0.39$ eV and $\Delta V_2 = 0.96$ eV under ambient conditions consistent with recent experiments~\cite{PhysRevLett.132.036702,Krempasky2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-038: On the other hand, at $15$~GPa, $\Delta V_1$ increases to $0.67$~eV and $\Delta V_2$ to $1.3$~eV.
elements.sent-2411-039: This corresponds to about 70\% increase in spin splitting of the first valence band and a 35\% increase for the second valence band when the system is under a pressure of $15$~GPa.
elements.sent-2411-040: We conclude that pressure enhances the nonrelativistic spin splitting in this material.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Figures/bands_vs_P.pdf} \caption{Electronic band structure of altermagnetic semiconductor $\alpha$-MnTe with $g$-wave symmetry of spin polarization in momentum space. The left and right panels show the spin-resolved band structure of bulk $\alpha$-MnTe at ambient pressure and $15$~GPa, respectively. $\Delta V_1$ and $\Delta V_2$ represent the spin subband splitting of the first and second valence bands, respectively.} \label{fig:bands_vs_P}
elements.sent-2411-041: Heisenberg exchange interactions-- In the inset of Fig.~\ref{fig:MnTe_Js (b), we present the Heisenberg exchange interactions as a function of distance, derived from DFT calculations of the total energy at ambient pressure and $15$~GPa.}
elements.sent-2411-042: We present results up to the 16th n.n.
elements.sent-2411-043: Heisenberg exchange interactions, $J_1$ to $J_{16}$, see also Table~\ref{tableI .}
elements.sent-2411-044: It is clear that increasing the pressure strengthens the Heisenberg exchange interactions for most exchange couplings.
elements.sent-2411-045: This enhancement can be attributed to the reduction in bond lengths, which improves the overlap of electron wavefunctions and consequently increases electron hopping.
elements.sent-2411-046: At ambient conditions, for the 2nd n.n., in-plane exchange interaction, our results predict an FM-type interaction, consistent with two experimental findings~\cite{PhysRevLett.133.156702,PhysRevB.73.104403 , while previous ab initio studies report an AFM-type interaction~\cite{PhysRevB.107.L100418,PhysRevMaterials.3.025403,https://doi.org/10.1002/apxr.202300050 .}}
elements.sent-2411-047: Thus, the magnetic ground state is an $A$-type AFM as it was shown in experiments.
elements.sent-2411-048: It is worth noting that the $3d$-orbital occupancy of the Mn$^{2+}$ ions in $\alpha$-MnTe is approximately $5.3$, indicating it is not exactly at half-filling.
elements.sent-2411-049: This slight deviation from half-filling can facilitate FM exchange interactions between in-plane Mn ions, as the electron configuration allows for some degree of spin alignment.
elements.sent-2411-050: We argue that this discrepancy between experiments and previous DFT calculations arise from using not enough number of magnetic configurations to map ab initio total energy to the Heisenberg Hamiltonian.
elements.sent-2411-051: Figure~\ref{fig:MnTe_Js (b) clearly shows that relying on only 18 magnetic configurations results in an incorrect AFM-type interaction for \( J_2 \) in our calculation as well.}
elements.sent-2411-052: However, as we increase the number of magnetic configurations, the sign of exchange interaction switches to the correct FM-type interaction, highlighting the importance of verifying the convergence of exchange interactions with respect to the number of magnetic configurations in complex AFM systems.
elements.sent-2411-053: Although the sign of $J_2$ in our calculations is in agreement with the experimental measurements, its amplitude is smaller~\cite{PhysRevLett.133.156702,PhysRevB.73.104403 .}
elements.sent-2411-054: We believe that this may arise from the limited number of Heisenberg exchange interactions chosen for fitting the experimental data.

elements.sent-2411-057: As we already mentioned, $\alpha$-MnTe is a prototype of altermagnetic systems.

elements.sent-2411-069: We use the following minimal spin Hamiltonian to describe the spin interactions in $\alpha$-MnTe, \begin{equation}\label{eqn:Hamiltonian} \mathcal{H}=-\sum_{i<j}J_{ij}\hat{\mathbf{S}}_{i}\cdot\hat{\mathbf{S}}_{j}-K\sum_{i}(\hat{\mathbf{S}}_i\cdot\hat{\mathbf{e}}_i)^{2}-\mu_{s}h_{0}\sum_{i}\hat{\mathbf{b}}\cdot\hat{\mathbf{S}}_{i},\end{equation} where $K>0$ is the single-ion uniaxial easy-axis magnetic anisotropy constant, $\hat{\mathbf{e _i$ is the magnetic anisotropy direction, and $\mu_{s}$ is the atomic magnetic moment.}}
elements.sent-2411-070: We assume a Zeeman-like interaction between localized magnetic moments and an external magnetic field with amplitude $h_{0}$ along the $\hat{\mathbf{b }$ direction.}
elements.sent-2411-071: The crystalline magnetic anisotropy, which arises from spin--orbit coupling, is very weak in $\alpha$-MnTe.
elements.sent-2411-072: Due to its small magnitude, it cannot be reliably determined either experimentally or through ab initio calculations.
elements.sent-2411-073: For our analysis, we adopt a value of $K = 6.25$~meV \cite{PhysRevB.73.104403,PhysRevB.107.L100418 .}
elements.sent-2411-074: In collinear uniaxial AFM systems, magnons can have two chiral $\alpha$ and $\beta$ eigenmodes, $\mathcal{H = \sum_{k (\omega_k^{\alpha \alpha_k^\dag \alpha_k +\omega_k^{\beta \beta_k^\dag \beta_k)$~\cite{10.1063/1.5109132 .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-075: In conventional $\mathcal{PT}$ symmetric AFM systems, these two chiral modes are usually degenerate, $\omega_k^{\alpha =\omega_k^{\beta}$.}
elements.sent-2411-076: However, in altermagnets, the chiral magnon bands split~\cite{PhysRevLett.131.256703 as was shown in a recent experiment on $\alpha$-MnTe~\cite{PhysRevLett.133.156702 .}}
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Figures/dispersionMnTe.pdf} \caption{Magnon dispersion relation of $\alpha$-MnTe in the ambient conditions (green lines) and under a compressive pressure of 15 GPa (purple lines). In the absence of magnetic field, chiral $\alpha$ (dashed lines) and $\beta$ (solid lines) magnon subbands are degenerate around the $\Gamma$ symmetry point, while the chiral degeneracy is lifted along the L-$\Gamma$-L symmetry path. See the SM for magnon dispersions in the presence of a magnetic field~\cite{SM}.} \label{fig:dispersionMnTe}

elements.sent-2411-082: In addition, we compute the magnetic susceptibility of the system using Monte Carlo calculations in Fig.~\ref{fig:susceptlibilityP0Pa .}

elements.sent-2411-086: This discrepancy is probably due to an underestimation of the exchange interaction parameter \(J_1\) in our calculations.
elements.sent-2411-087: Finally, from inverse magnetic susceptibility calculations, see the SM~\cite{SM , we obtain the AFM Curie--Weiss temperature $\Theta$ via phenomenological Curie--Weiss law $\chi_z^{-1 \propto (T + \Theta)$ \cite{Mugiraneza_2022 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-088: Under ambient conditions, we find $|\Theta| \approx 620$~K, which is slightly higher than the previous experimental values of $585$~K reported in Refs.~\cite{doi:10.1021/j100822a006,doi:10.1143/JPSJ.18.356 .}
elements.sent-2411-089: Under applied pressure, however, $|\Theta|$ significantly increases to around 3100 K.
elements.sent-2411-090: These results are in agreement with the typical frustration index $|\Theta|/T_{\rm{N }\propto 2-5$ of unfrustrated 3$d$ transition metals~\cite{Mugiraneza_2022 .}}
elements.sent-2411-091: Concluding remarks-- $\alpha$-MnTe is an altermagnetic semiconductor with robust piezomagnetic properties, making it a promising candidate for spintronic applications.
elements.sent-2411-092: By resolving the in-plane Heisenberg exchange interaction $J_2$ discrepancy between the experimental findings and prior DFT calculations, we highlight the critical role of magnetic configurations in accurately modeling complex antiferromagnets.
elements.sent-2411-093: Furthermore, we identify \( J_{10a(b) \) as the primary driver behind the chiral magnon splitting in $\alpha$-MnTe.}
elements.sent-2411-094: Notably, applied pressure modulates both the sign and magnitude of the Heisenberg exchange interactions, enhancing spin polarization and chiral band splitting in both electronic and magnonic spectra.
elements.sent-2411-095: This work underscores the importance of detailed spin interaction analysis in advancing the physics of altermagnetic materials for next-generation spintronics technologies.
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Figures/Susceptibility_MnTe_Large.pdf} \caption{Directional magnetic susceptibilities at the absence (a) and presence of pressure (b). In our atomistic simulations, we model a cubic system with dimensions of \( 10 \, \text{nm} \times 10 \, \text{nm} \times 10 \, \text{nm} \), with periodic boundary conditions. } \label{fig:susceptlibilityP0Pa}

elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Figures/dispersionMnTeExtField_insets.pdf} \caption{Effect of external magnetic field on magnon dispersion relation of $\alpha$-MnTe in the presence and absence of pressure. (a) in the absence of a magnetic field $h_0= 0$. (b) $h_0=-5$ T. (c) $h_0=+5$ T. We find a spin-flop field $h_{\rm{sf}}=5.7$ T at $P=0$ Pa and $h_{\rm{sf}}=7.6$ T at $P=15$ GPa. It is worthy to note that in the presence of a magnetic field, two chiral magnon branches cross each other. Insets show the dispersions around the $\Gamma$ symmetry point.} \label{fig:dispersionMnTeExtField}
elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Figures/dispersionMnTeExtFieldDiff_inset.pdf} \caption{Effect of magnetic field on splitting of chiral bands in the absence and presence of the pressure. (a) $h_0= 0$. (b) $h_0=-5$ T. (c) $h_0=+5$ T. Insets show the splitting around the $\Gamma$ symmetry point.} \label{fig:dispersionMnTeExtFieldDiff}
elements.figure-2411-008: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Figures/InverseSusceptibility_MnTe_inset_Large.pdf} \caption{Temperature dependence of the inverse longitudinal magnetic susceptibility, calculated using Monte Carlo simulations. The data are fitted with the antiferromagnetic Curie–Weiss law, $\chi_z^{-1} \propto (T + \Theta)$. Main plot: At ambient pressure ($P = 0$), the Curie–Weiss constant is $\Theta \approx -620$ K. Inset: Under compressive strain ($P = 15$ GPa), the constant increases to $\Theta \approx -3100$ K.} \label{fig:InverseSusceptlibilityP0Pa}
elements.figure-2411-009: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Figures/MnTe_GroundStateP0Pa_J2.pdf} \caption{Ground state magnetic configuration for $\alpha$-MnTe at ambient conditions ($P = 0$) with only $J_1<0$ (AFM-type) and $J_2>0$ (FM-type) Heisenberg exchange interactions. System shows an $A$-type AFM ground state. } \label{fig:GroundStateP=0Pa}
elements.figure-2411-010: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Figures/MnTe_GroundStateP15GPa_J2.pdf} \caption{Ground state magnetic configuration for $\alpha$-MnTe under compressive strain ($P = 15$ GPa) with only $\{J_1, J_2\} < 0$, i.e., both interlayer and in-plane interactions are AFM type. System shows a frustrated AFM state.} \label{fig:GroundStateP=15Pa}
elements.figure-2411-011: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Figures/MnTe_GroundStateP15GPa_J3.pdf} \caption{Ground state magnetic configuration for $\alpha$-MnTe under compressive strain($P = 15$ GPa) with $\{J_1, J_2, J_3\} < 0$, i.e., all AFM type. $J_3<0$ stabilizes an $A$-type AFM ground state.} \label{fig:GroundStateP=15Pa-J3}
elements.sent-2411-099: The origin of the $A$-type antiferromagnetic ordering, characterized by ferromagnetic layers coupling antiferromagnetically, in the prototype semiconductor altermagnet $\alpha$-MnTe has been a topic of ongoing debate.
elements.sent-2411-100: Experimentally, $\alpha$-MnTe exhibits an in-plane ferromagnetic exchange interaction, whereas previous ab initio calculations predicted an antiferromagnetic interaction.
elements.sent-2411-101: In this paper, we resolve this discrepancy by considering an expanded set of magnetic configurations, which reveals a ferromagnetic in-plane exchange interaction in agreement with experimental findings.
elements.sent-2411-102: Additionally, we demonstrate that the 10th nearest-neighbor exchange interaction is directionally dependent, inducing a nonrelativistic chiral splitting in the magnon bands, as recently observed experimentally.
elements.sent-2411-103: We further show that applying a compressive strain may significantly enhance both nonrelativistic spin and chiral magnon splittings.
elements.sent-2411-104: The strain can also change the sign of the in-plane exchange interaction.
elements.sent-2411-105: Computing magnetic susceptibility, we show that strain enhances the N{\'e el temperature, significantly.}
elements.sent-2411-106: Our results highlight the critical importance of convergence in the number of magnetic configurations for spin interactions in antiferromagnetic materials.
elements.sent-2411-107: Introduction-- Antiferromagnetic (AFM) interactions give rise to a diverse range of AFM classes, ranging from different collinear structures to various exotic noncollinear and frustrated structures~\cite{RevModPhys.90.015005,Parkin,dal2024antiferromagnetic .}
elements.sent-2411-108: A class of collinear AFM systems, in which combined inversion or translation and time-reversal symmetry ($\mathcal{IT}$ or $t\mathcal{T}$) is broken while combined crystal-rotation and time-reversal symmetry are retained, has recently been identified in various AFM materials, both theoretically ~\cite{noda2016momentum,naka2019spin,doi:10.7566/JPSJ.88.123702,PhysRevB.99.184432,vsmejkal2020crystal,PhysRevB.102.014422,PhysRevMaterials.5.014409,doi:10.1021/acs.jpcc.1c02653,mazin2021prediction,PhysRevLett.126.127701,Ma_2021,PhysRevX.12.040501,PhysRevX.12.011028,PhysRevX.12.031042,PhysRevB.102.014422,PhysRevLett.131.256703,Zunger-review,yuan2023uncovering,2024ApPhL.124c0503Y,PhysRevX.14.011019,S_dequist_2024,Cheong_2024,KIMEL2024172039,https://doi.org/10.1002/adfm.202409327, jungwirth2024,jungwirth2024supefluid3healtermagnets and experimentally~\cite{Krempasky2024,PhysRevLett.132.036702,PhysRevB.109.115102,doi:10.1126/sciadv.adj4883,Reimers_2024, https://doi.org/10.1002/advs.202406529,ding2024largebandsplittinggwavetype .}}
elements.sent-2411-109: These properties lead to the preservation of band degeneracy in the center of the Brillouin zone while removing the Kramers degeneracy in certain regions of the magnetic Brillouin zone.
elements.sent-2411-110: This class of nonrelativistic spin-split collinear AFM materials has been termed altermagnetism.
elements.sent-2411-111: Therefore, in altermagnets, despite the absence of net magnetization, the electronic (magnonic) band structures display direction-dependent spin (chiral) splitting.
elements.sent-2411-112: Altermagnets generally belong to a broader class of nonrelativistic spin-split collinear AFM systems, which also include AFM half-metals, where the band degeneracy can even be broken at the center of the Brillouin zone \cite{PhysRevLett.133.216701,doi:10.1073/pnas.1715465115,PhysRevLett.74.1171,osti_4642614 .}
elements.figure-2411-012: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Figures/crystal_Js-2.pdf} \caption{(a) Crystal structure of $\alpha$-MnTe. We present several important spin exchange interactions: the 10th n.n. interactions ($J_{10,a}$ and $J_{10,b}$) that lead to altermagnetism, as well as 1st. n.n. $J_1$ and 2nd n.n. $J_2$ that lead to $A$-type AFM order. Large purple spheres represent Mn atoms and small cyan spheres represent Te atoms. (b) 2nd n.n. normalized Heisenberg exchange interaction $J_2$, plotted against the number of magnetic configurations, starting from 18. Initially, $J_2$ is negative (AFM), but it transitions to positive (FM) and stabilizes as the number of configurations increases. The inset of (b) represents the normalized Heisenberg exchange interactions at ambient pressure $P=0$ and $P=15$ GPa.} \label{fig:MnTe_Js}

elements.sent-2411-125: Computational methods-- To calculate the electronic band structures, the total energy, and consequently spin interactions, we use the projected augmented wave (PAW) method, as implemented in the Vienna ab initio simulation package (VASP)~\cite{PhysRevB.59.1758 .}
elements.sent-2411-126: The plane wave expansion employs a cutoff energy of 550 eV.

elements.sent-2411-128: Furthermore, we include a Hubbard correction of $U = 4$ eV, as estimated in Ref.~\cite{Mosleh2023 , to improve the treatment of electron-electron interactions.}
elements.sent-2411-129: To compute Heisenberg exchange interactions $\Tilde{J _{ij}$, between localized spins $\mathbf{S _{i =S \hat{\mathbf{S _{i}$, where $S$ is the spin length and $\hat{\mathbf{S _{i}$ denotes the spin direction; we employ an implicit approach, named total energy mapping, by fitting a classical Heisenberg model $\mathcal{H =-\sum_{i<j J_{ij}\hat{\mathbf{S _{i}\cdot\hat{\mathbf{S _{j}$, where ${J _{ij =S^2\Tilde{J _{ij}$ are normalized Heisenberg exchange interactions, to the total energy derived from calculations of the electronic structure across numerous magnetic configurations~\cite{, Alaei_2023, Mosleh2023, RevModPhys.95.035004 .}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-133: Spin-resolved electronic band structure-- In altermagnets, Kramers degeneracy is lifted, leading to a momentum-dependent splitting of spin subbands in the electronic band structure.

elements.sent-2411-169: Concluding remarks-- $\alpha$-MnTe serves as a prototypical altermagnetic semiconductor, distinguished by its robust piezomagnetic properties.
elements.sent-2411-170: These characteristics make it a compelling candidate for advanced spintronics applications.
elements.sent-2411-171: In this study, we address two open questions: the origin of $A$-type AFM order in this material and the chiral split of magnon bands.
elements.sent-2411-172: Furthermore, we identify \( J_{10a(b) \) as the primary driver behind the splitting of chiral magnons in $\alpha$-MnTe.}
elements.sent-2411-173: This work underscores the importance of a detailed spin interaction analysis in advancing the physics of altermagnetic materials for next-generation spintronics technologies.
